citation: Bourgon V, Duval FG, Forest G. Circadian variation in coaches' decision-making in the National Football League's evening games. Chronobiology International. 2024.
abstract: This study explored whether NFL coaches show circadian variation in their decision-making on fourth down when traveling through time zones. Data from visiting teams across 20 NFL regular seasons (2000–2020) were analyzed (n=5,360 games). Decision-making was measured as the percentage of offensive plays on fourth down. A factorial ANCOVA and moderation analysis were used to examine whether travel direction and time of game moderated fourth-down decision patterns. In evening games, coaches traveling westward called significantly more offensive plays on fourth down (M=14.90%) compared to teams traveling within the same time zone (M=12.20%, p=0.03) or eastward (M=11.35%, p=0.007). The moderation analysis showed that only in evening games did greater westward longitudinal degrees traveled predict higher percentages of fourth-down offensive plays, with the effect significant for games starting at 16:24h or later. No significant effect of travel direction was found in day games. Fourth-down rates also increased significantly over the seasons studied. The study suggests that circadian misalignment from westward travel may influence coaching decision-making, not just player performance, in professional football.
tldr: NFL coaches traveling westward for evening games call significantly more fourth-down offensive plays than those traveling in other directions, suggesting that circadian misalignment from time-zone travel impairs coaching decision-making — not just player performance — in professional football.
methods: Retrospective analysis of 20 NFL regular seasons (2000–2020); n=5,360 games; data from profootballreference.com and nfl.com; visiting team statistics extracted; dependent variable: percentage of offensive plays on fourth down; independent variables: travel direction (westward/same TZ/eastward), longitudinal degrees traveled, time of game (day vs. evening, and continuous), year of season; factorial ANCOVA (Time of game × Direction of travel) with year as covariate; moderation analysis via PROCESS 3.1; post-hoc Tukey t-tests for evening games.
findings: In evening games, westward-traveling teams had significantly higher fourth-down offensive play rates (14.90%) vs. same-TZ (12.20%, p=0.03) and eastward travelers (11.35%, p=0.007). No travel direction effect in day games. Moderation analysis: greater westward longitudinal degrees predicted more fourth-down plays only in games starting after 16:24h. Fourth-down offensive play rate increased significantly across seasons (covariate effect p<0.001). Effect size small (0.2% variance explained) but statistically significant.
limitations: No individual coach sleep or chronotype data available; game-situation confounders (score, time remaining, field position, opponent strength) not controlled; fourth-down decisions represent only one type of coaching decision; small explained variance (0.2%); exploratory study design; cannot establish causality.
practicalImplications: Sleep and circadian rhythm interventions for traveling NFL teams should target not only players but also coaching and support staff. Teams traveling westward for evening games should implement proactive circadian adaptation strategies (light exposure, sleep scheduling, melatonin) to protect cognitive decision-making quality. NFL schedulers should consider giving earlier game-time slots to teams traveling westward through multiple time zones.
athleteDev: Beyond physical performance, circadian misalignment from westward travel in American football can impair the complex cognitive and strategic decision-making of coaches. Staff education on the cognitive effects of jet lag and proactive circadian management should be standard practice for any team engaging in cross-timezone travel.
rtp: Circadian disruption from westward travel in evening games may impair coaching decision-making, which in high-leverage situations could affect return-to-play or in-game management decisions for players. Medical and performance staff should account for circadian state when making high-stakes decisions following long-distance westward travel.

citation: Han Z, Sun P, Chen T, Dong Y. Club-based vs. hospital-guided rehabilitation after ACL reconstruction in football participants: a two-year prospective cohort study. Frontiers in Sports and Active Living. 2025;7:1641750.
abstract: This prospective cohort study compared two-year clinical outcomes and return to sport (RTS) following ACL reconstruction in football players randomized to either hospital-guided rehabilitation (n=20) or club-based sport-specific rehabilitation (n=20). Participants were football players and enthusiasts aged 18–40 who underwent primary ACL reconstruction at Huashan Hospital, Fudan University (2018–2021). Hospital-guided rehabilitation emphasized standardized joint function recovery supervised by orthopedic surgeons and physical therapists. Club-based rehabilitation was led by strength and conditioning coaches at the Dalian Football Base and incorporated high-intensity, football-specific tasks (agility drills, explosive power, simulated match scenarios, psychological readiness) at greater frequency and commitment. At two-year follow-up, the club-based group demonstrated significantly better Tegner Activity Scores (8.5 vs. 7.2, p=0.006), Marx Activity Rating Scale scores (12.7 vs. 10.6, p=0.019), and ACL-RSI scores (73.3 vs. 54.3, p=0.003). Time to return to training games (12.4 vs. 18.3 weeks, p=0.042) and official matches (19.0 vs. 26.5 weeks, p=0.002) was significantly shorter in the club-based group. No significant differences were found in ROM, joint stability, IKDC, or Lysholm scores. Return-to-competitive-sport rate was 90% (club) vs. 65% (hospital). One reinjury occurred in each group. Club-based rehabilitation produced superior subjective functional outcomes and faster RTS without increasing reinjury risk.
tldr: Club-based sport-specific rehabilitation after ACL reconstruction in football players produces superior subjective function, greater psychological readiness (ACL-RSI), and significantly faster return to both training and competitive matches compared to hospital-guided rehabilitation, without increasing reinjury risk over a two-year follow-up.
methods: Prospective cohort study; N=40 football players and enthusiasts (18–40 years) with primary ACL reconstruction (Huashan Hospital, 2018–2021); two groups: hospital-guided (n=20) and club-based (n=20); baseline characteristics comparable; outcome measures: Tegner, Marx, IKDC, Lysholm, ACL-RSI scores; objective clinical assessments (swelling, ROM, stability); return-to-sport timing and reinjury rate at 2-year follow-up; statistical analysis: independent-samples t-test, Mann-Whitney U, chi-square; alpha=0.05.
findings: Club-based group: significantly higher Tegner (8.5 vs. 7.2, p=0.006), Marx (12.7 vs. 10.6, p=0.019), and ACL-RSI (73.3 vs. 54.3, p=0.003) scores. No significant differences in IKDC or Lysholm. Return to training games: 12.4 vs. 18.3 weeks (p=0.042). Return to official matches: 19.0 vs. 26.5 weeks (p=0.002). Competitive RTS rate: 90% vs. 65%. No significant differences in ROM, stability, or reinjury rate. One reinjury per group.
limitations: Small sample size (n=20 per group); non-random group assignment based on rehabilitation modality choice; higher proportion of professional footballers in club group (55% vs. 30%, p=0.110) may confound results; residual confounding from graft type and surgical technique differences; club-based model requires greater time, cost, and athlete commitment, limiting scalability; single-center design; generalizability to female athletes or other sports not established.
practicalImplications: Football programs should consider integrating sport-specific, club-based rehabilitation elements after ACL reconstruction, particularly for competitive players who need earlier return to training and matches. Psychological readiness (ACL-RSI) should be explicitly assessed and targeted — the large gap in ACL-RSI scores (73.3 vs. 54.3) highlights that standard hospital rehab may inadequately prepare athletes psychologically for return to play. Criteria-based RTS should include functional and psychological benchmarks, not only clinical clearance.
athleteDev: For developing football athletes, ACL rehabilitation should move beyond joint-level recovery toward sport-specific functional restoration. Club-based programs incorporating agility, explosive power, match simulations, and psychological readiness interventions from early rehabilitation phases are more effective at restoring competitive capacity. Strength and conditioning coaches should be integrated into the rehabilitation team from early stages post-surgery.
rtp: Club-based sport-specific rehabilitation achieves competitive return to football 7.5 weeks faster than hospital-guided rehab (19.0 vs. 26.5 weeks to official match) with a higher competitive RTS rate (90% vs. 65%) at 2 years post-ACL reconstruction. Return-to-play criteria should include sport-specific functional tests (Tegner, Marx, ACL-RSI) alongside traditional clinical measures. Psychological readiness (ACL-RSI) was substantially lower in hospital-guided patients, suggesting this group requires additional psychological support before competitive clearance.

citation: Does Overexertion Correlate With Increased Injury? The Relationship Between Player Workload and Soft Tissue Injury in Professional American Football Players Using Wearable Technology.
Drill-Specific Head Impacts in Collegiate Football Practice: Implications for Reducing Friendly Fire Exposure.
Ferrari F, Stein R, Milani M. Electrocardiographic diversity in football players: how distinct ethnic patterns shape athletes' hearts. European Journal of Preventive Cardiology. 2026;33:6–7.
abstract: This interview-format editorial presents findings from a series of four landmark studies examining ECG patterns in over 6,000 male Brazilian soccer (football) players conducted by Ferrari, Stein, and colleagues over two decades. Key findings across the series: (1) 97% of ECGs were normal, with only 3% showing abnormalities — lower than African cohorts (23%), partially attributed to Brazil's high ethnic admixture; (2) early repolarization (ER) was common (40%), especially in Black (48%) and Mixed-race (41%) athletes, with the classic J-wave/ascending ST-segment morphology predominating and no sudden cardiac deaths during mean 4-year follow-up; (3) the 'African/Afro-Caribbean' ECG pattern was rare in Brazilian players and was not associated with structural abnormalities on echocardiography when present; (4) ECG abnormalities become more common with age (especially 26–35 years). Mixed-race athletes remain underrepresented in current international ECG interpretation guidelines. The authors advocate for current international criteria (Drezner et al., 2017) as effective when correctly applied, and call for expanded ECG databases in admixed populations. Inferolateral T-wave inversion should never be dismissed even with normal echocardiogram.
tldr: A two-decade series studying ECGs in over 6,000 Brazilian footballers found that 97% were normal, early repolarization is common and benign in this population, the 'African/Afro-Caribbean' ECG pattern is rare due to Brazil's high ethnic admixture, and current international criteria are effective when correctly applied — but mixed-race athletes remain underrepresented in guidelines.
methods: Interview-format editorial summarizing findings from four previously published studies (B-Pro Foot ECG study series); combined sample >6,000 male Brazilian soccer players from clubs across Brazil; ECG screening with imaging correlates (echocardiography, cardiac MRI); analysis by ethnicity (White, Black, Mixed-race); longitudinal follow-up (mean 4 years).
findings: 97% of ECGs normal (3% abnormal); early repolarization prevalence 40% overall (48% Black, 41% Mixed-race); 'African/Afro-Caribbean' ECG pattern rare in Brazilian players due to genetic admixture; no sudden cardiac deaths during 4-year follow-up; ECG abnormalities increase with age (especially 26–35 years); Mixed-race athletes' ECG findings intermediate between White and Black athletes; inferolateral T-wave inversion warrants cardiac MRI even with normal echo; early repolarization benign in athletic context.
limitations: Limited to male athletes; no female athlete data in published series; interview/editorial format limits primary data presentation; single-country (Brazil) sample may limit generalizability; ethnic self-identification heterogeneity within 'Black' and 'Mixed-race' categories; no data on non-soccer football codes.
practicalImplications: Team physicians screening football players should not over-interpret early repolarization patterns — these are common, training-related, and benign in this population. Inferolateral T-wave inversion should always prompt cardiac MRI evaluation, even when echocardiogram is normal. Current international ECG criteria (Drezner 2017) are effective and appropriate when applied correctly. ECG interpretation programs should account for ethnic diversity and admixture.
athleteDev: Pre-participation ECG screening in football programs involving ethnically diverse or admixed athlete populations should use current international criteria adapted for the specific population. Longitudinal cardiac monitoring should intensify after age 26 when ECG abnormality rates increase.
rtp: Football players with abnormal ECG findings — particularly inferolateral T-wave inversion — should not be cleared for return to sport without cardiac MRI evaluation, regardless of echocardiogram result. Early repolarization patterns, including J-waves with ascending ST-segments, do not require restriction from sport participation. Ethnic background and admixture should inform ECG interpretation during return-to-play cardiac clearance protocols.
